coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a novel Z-scheme Bi2MoO6/GQDs/MoS2 hierarchical nanocomposite for the photo-oxidation of ofloxacin and photoreduction of Cr(VI) as aqueous pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Sandip
Adhikari, Sangeeta
Choi, Sangki
Lee, Yunho
Kim, Do-Heyoung
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalyst, which was further sensitized using graphene quantum dots (GQDs), was developed to realize multiple photocatalytic applications in antibiotic remediation and Cr(VI) reduction. A simple chemical mixing procedure was used to prepare the GQD-MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst, which was comprehensively characterized to confirm the structural purity, morphology, and distribution of GQDs on the MoS2/BMO catalyst. The enhanced charge separation and channeling of fast electron migration were confirmed by photocurrent and impedance measurements obtained for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO ternary catalyst system. The photoefficiency observed for the degradation of ofloxacin and reduction of Cr(VI) was 99.5% and 97.2% after 100 and 30 min, respectively, of photochemical reaction under visible-light irradiation. The reaction rate with 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO was 14.3- and 1.8-fold higher than that using pristine Bi2MoO6 and 30 MoS2/BMO photocatalysts, respectively. Persulfate (3 mM) improved the photo-oxidation of ofloxacin, with > 80% degradation occurring in the first 20 min. The excellent photocatalytic performance is attributed to the improved light absorption owing to the GQDs and the promotion of charge transfer between Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 via GQDs through a Z-scheme mechanism and the electron-attracting properties of the GQDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and graphene quantum dots
Charge-transfer mechanism in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO ternary-composite photocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the prepared photocatalysts: (A) Bi2MoO6 (BMO), (B) MoS2 (inset shows the high-resolution image), and (C) 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO. (D) HR-TEM images of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, (E) MoS2, and (F) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (inset shows the high-resolution image and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns).
(A) X-ray diffraction patterns and (B) Raman spectra of Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts.

(A) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms obtained for pristine Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. X-ray photoelectron spectra obtained for the as-prepared 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts: (B) survey spectra, and (C) C1s, (D) O1s, (E) Mo3d and S2s, and (F) Bi4f and S2p spectra.

(A) UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra obtained for Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts. (B) Tauc plots of MoS2 and Bi2MoO6 were used to calculate the band gap energies. (C) Photocurrent-response spectra and (D) EIS Nyquist plots were obtained for Bi2MoO6 and MoS2, as well as the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts.

(A) Photocatalytic degradation and (B) kinetic profile of ofloxacin using different photocatalysts prepared with various concentrations of MoS2 in MoS2/BMO. (C) Photocatalytic degradation and (D) kinetic profile of ofloxacin using different photocatalysts prepared with various concentrations of GQDs in 30 MoS2/BMO under visible-light radiation. (E) Photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin under the influence of persulfate using the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst (inset: kinetic profile). (F) Cycling experiments using ofloxacin and the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst with persulfate.

(A) Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). (B) Pseudo-first-order kinetic plots and (C) kinetic rate constants for Bi2MoO6, MoS2, 30 MoS2/BMO, and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO. (D) Recyclability study on 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO during the photoreduction of Cr(VI).

(A) Effects of different scavengers on the photodegradation of ofloxacin using the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst (inset: plot of the kinetic rate constant versus scavengers). (B) EPR spectra for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO catalyst with respect to irradiated visible light for various time intervals. (C) Reaction mechanism for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite catalyst system, showing the feasible Z-scheme mechanism.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a novel Z-scheme Bi2MoO6/GQDs/MoS2 hierarchical nanocomposite for the photo-oxidation of ofloxacin and photoreduction of Cr(VI) as aqueous pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalyst, which was further sensitized using graphene quantum dots (GQDs), was developed to realize multiple photocatalytic applications in antibiotic remediation and Cr(VI) reduction. A simple chemical mixing procedure was used to prepare the GQD-MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst, which was comprehensively characterized to confirm the structural purity, morphology, and distribution of GQDs on the MoS2/BMO catalyst. The enhanced charge separation and channeling of fast electron migration were confirmed by photocurrent and impedance measurements obtained for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO ternary catalyst system. The photoefficiency observed for the degradation of ofloxacin and reduction of Cr(VI) was 99.5% and 97.2% after 100 and 30 min, respectively, of photochemical reaction under visible-light irradiation. The reaction rate with 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO was 14.3- and 1.8-fold higher than that using pristine Bi2MoO6 and 30 MoS2/BMO photocatalysts, respectively. Persulfate (3 mM) improved the photo-oxidation of ofloxacin, with > 80% degradation occurring in the first 20 min. The excellent photocatalytic performance is attributed to the improved light absorption owing to the GQDs and the promotion of charge transfer between Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 via GQDs through a Z-scheme mechanism and the electron-attracting properties of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is a reliable, feasible, and cost-effective catalytic method that has recently emerged as one of the most promising strategies to address global environmental pollution and energy problems. Recently, micropollutants in drinking water and other aquatic ecosystems have created alarming concerns, posing a significant risk to human health and the environment. For example, antibiotics such as sulfonamides, cephalosporin, quinolones, and fluoroquinolone are micropollutants often found in groundwater at concentrations up to 5100 ng/L or higher and are discharged from wastewater treatment facilities, hospitals, residential areas, and the agricultural, aquacultural, and medicinal sectors [1,2]. Ofloxacin, a fluoroquinolone belonging to the third generation of the quinolone family of antibiotics, is a typical pseudo-persistent organic pollutant. Excessive exposure to ofloxacin through water supply can cause renal failure and allergic reactions [3]. In addition to antibiotics, heavy metals contribute to micro-pollution. In particular, Cr(VI), which is released from industrial sectors such as chrome plating, pigment manufacturing, tanning, electroplating, and wood preservation [5], is among the top-ten most harmful metal pollutants in the world [4]. However, owing to its ease of removal, the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is an efficient process that can be used to eliminate Cr(VI) from wastewater systems [6].

Several methods have been utilized to treat water contaminated with antibiotics and Cr(VI); however, very few studies have considered the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and antibiotics through adsorption [7], advanced oxidation [8], electrochemical oxidation [9], and photocatalytic oxidation and reduction [10]. Among these methods, photocatalytic oxidation/reduction is one of the most inexpensive methods reported to date. Chen et al. studied the degradation of ofloxacin using a perylene-diimide–sunlight photocatalytic system [11]. However, the low charge-separation efficiency and high carrier-transport activation energy of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs restrict the photocatalytic performance [12]. The systematic design of ordered and efficient photocatalysts is an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic activity through semiconductors. The efficiency of light absorption and carrier transition in novel synthetic semiconductors is closely correlated with their photocatalytic efficiency, wherein high light absorption and a high carrier-diffusion rate are critical for optimizing the photocatalytic conversion ability of the synthetic materials [13,14]. Short carrier lifetimes and low intrinsic carrier-transport activation energy are two major drawbacks of the designed photocatalysts. In addition to approaches such as doping, morphology tuning [15], crystal-facet control [16], and metal loading [17,18], heterostructure-based nanostructures [19] have attracted considerable attention owing to their ability to extend the lifetime of the excited-state electron–hole pairs while decreasing charge recombination, thereby opening up a new avenue for achieving excellent photocatalytic performance [20–22].

Configuring the photocatalyst dimensions can also influence catalyst performance and almost overlook the material characteristics. Recently, the development of a hierarchical heterojunction with quantum-dot sensitization over the structure has been shown to be effective for increasing the lifetime of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs [23]. One of the advantages of hierarchical structures include their large specific surface area owing to the self-assembled nanostructures [24]. The reactive sites over these hierarchical structures can be further increased from the deposition of quantum dots. Quantum dots can accelerate photochemical reactions through decreased migration distance of the photogenerated charges owing to their small particle sizes [25]. Bi-based mixed oxides such as Bi2MoO6 have emerged as effective alternative materials to traditional photocatalysts owing to their non-toxic nature, remarkable electronic structure, and optimal band gap (≈ 2.4 eV) for visible-light absorption. Composites containing C3N4 [26], CdS [27], CuAl2O4 [28], Bi2O2CO3/Bi2MoO6 [29], and AgI/BiVO4 [30] have been reported to compensate for the charge recombination phenomena in a single photocatalytic system. Similarly, MoS2, which has a band gap of ≈ 1.75 eV, is a suitable candidate exhibiting photocatalytic activity owing to its absorption in the UV and visible-light regions [31]. In addition, MoS2 has recently become an attractive material in photocatalysis, photodetection, photovoltaic cells, electrocatalysis, and microbiology owing to its high room-temperature mobility (410 cm2 V−1 s−1) and outstanding optical properties [32]. Moreover, the S–Mo–S crystal lattice allows the unsaturated S and Mo atoms to contribute to the unique “edge action” of MoS2 [33]. In addition to a high specific surface area, a short charge-transfer pathway and optimal surface dispersion are important; to this end, zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials are considered promising catalysts [34]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are 0D nanomaterials with the advantages of low cost, high conductivity, tunable band gap, and excellent photochemical properties, and are used for environmental remediation, CO2 reduction, and photoelectrochemical water splitting [35]. Heterostructured materials with GQD sensitization are prominent owing to their optimum redox potential and high-load carrier-separation properties. Several recent studies have reported heterostructures with GQDs, including BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 [36], ZnO/Ag/GQD [37], BiOCl/BiVO4/N-GQD [38], and others [39–41], which exhibited good photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical activities.

Based on the above perspective, in this study, ternary photocatalytic systems composed of Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 that incorporate GQDs were prepared, and their synergistic material properties and influence over photocatalytic oxidation and reduction reactions were investigated. A GQD-sensitized MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalyst system was developed using a combined hydrothermal and solution-mixing method. Physicochemical characterization of the composites with varying compositions was carried out to explore and analyze their structural, morphological, functional, and surface-state properties. Ofloxacin and Cr(VI) were investigated as a micropollutant and heavy metal, respectively, for photo-oxidation and photoreduction under visible-light irradiation. The redox reaction and active species in the ternary-composite system were determined by trapping and probe-based investigations, which elucidated the photocatalytic mechanism for the remediation of ofloxacin and Cr(VI) reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and GQDs is described in the Supporting Information.

A simple bath-evaporation process was carried out to develop the Bi2MoO6/MoS2/GQDs catalyst. Initially, a MoS2 suspension was prepared in an aqueous ethanol solution (50% v/v ethanol and deionized water) using an appropriate amount of MoS2, followed by ultrasonication for 1h to achieve a homogeneous suspension (Sol-A). An ethanolic Bi2MoO6 suspension was prepared by following the procedure used to prepare Sol-A, denoted as Sol-B. The MoS2 suspension (Sol-A) was added dropwise to the Bi2MoO6 suspension with continuous stirring (Sol-B). The resulting homogeneous suspension was placed on a heating plate at 60 °C to evaporate the solvent and obtain the dry catalyst. Different weight ratios (10–50 wt% MoS2) of the Bi2MoO6/MoS2 catalyst were prepared by varying the amount of the individual catalysts in the suspension. The composite catalysts were designated as 10 MoS2/BMO, 20 MoS2/BMO, 30 MoS2/BMO, 40 MoS2/BMO, and 50 MoS2/BMO, where the numbers represent the weight percentage of MoS2 in the catalyst. Furthermore, to prepare the Bi2MoO6/MoS2/GQD catalyst with different loadings of GQDs, 3, 5, and 10 mL of the 100 mL solution was added dropwise to Sol-B and stirred for 1 h. Sol-A was then added to the suspension to form the Bi2MoO6/MoS2/GQD catalyst suspension, which was isolated following a process similar to that employed for the Bi2MoO6/MoS2 catalyst. Since 30 MoS2/BMO was the optimized catalyst composition determined during our catalytic experiments, the GQD loading step was carried out using this catalyst composition. The ternary catalysts were designated as 3, 5, and 10 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO according to the GQD loading. The catalysts were not treated further and used as-prepared unless mentioned otherwise.

Details of the instrumentation used in the physicochemical analysis of the developed photocatalysts are provided in the Supporting Information. In addition, photocatalytic activity studies, reusability studies, and photoelectrochemical measurements are described in the Supporting Information (section 1.3).

The morphologies of the as-prepared photocatalysts were analyzed using high-resolution FESEM and TEM. Figure S1 illustrates the FESEM images of the pristine, binary, and ternary-composite photocatalysts, with the insets showing the corresponding high-resolution images. According to the FESEM images, the GQDs were untraceable in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite owing to their low amount. Additionally, the small size of the quantum dots rendered them undetectable using FESEM. Therefore, HR-TEM was performed to further analyze the deposition of the GQDs on the surface of the catalyst and to understand their arrangement and distribution on the catalyst surface.

The TEM images of pristine Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 are shown in Fig. 1
A and 1B, respectively. Pristine Bi2MoO6 exhibited a hierarchical flower-like morphology with sharp particle edges on the surface appearing from the random rearrangement of thin plates. However, the ultrathin nanosheets of MoS2 (inset) resembled petals that self-assembled to form a flower-like structure (Fig. 1 B); this morphology was also observed in the FESEM images. The TEM image of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO is shown in Fig. 1 C, which displays the interconnection between both the flowers in contact between the Bi2MoO6 plates and the ultrathin nanosheets. Since the quantum dots are very small, they are not visible in the low-resolution TEM image. However, they are apparent in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1 D). As can be seen, the GQDs are well distributed on the surface of the Bi2MoO6 plates and MoS2 nanosheets. The lattice fringes of the Bi2MoO6 plates are also observable, and the calculated d-spacing was ≈ 0.276 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of Bi2MoO6. The HR-TEM image of MoS2 is displayed in Fig. 1 E; the calculated lattice spacing was 0.27 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of MoS2. Furthermore, the GQDs had various sizes, with localized lattice fringes of graphene nanosheets having an in-plane lattice parameter of ≈ 0.24 nm, which were attributed to the (1120) plane. In addition, hexagonal lattice fringes were observed, and the corresponding selected-area electron diffraction pattern indicated the crystalline nature of the GQDs. The GQDs were attached to the surface of the particles, which is beneficial for the charge-transfer process during photocatalysis.

The structural properties of pristine Bi2MoO6, MoS2, 30 MoS2/BMO, and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO were analyzed using X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2
A and 2B, respectively. Fig. 2 A shows the characteristic diffraction peaks of Bi2MoO6 and MoS2, which can be assigned to the orthorhombic (JCPDS Card No. 76–2388) and hexagonal crystal phases, respectively. The distinctive peaks of Bi2MoO6 occur at 2θ values of 10.8° (020), 28.2° (131), 32.6° (002), 33.1° (060), 36.0° (151), 46.1° (202), 47.1° (260), 55.5° (331), and 58.4° (262) [42]. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of hexagonal MoS2 appear at 2θ = 14.0° (002), 33.3° (100), 39.6° (103), 49.3° (105), and 59.0° (110), which match well with those of JCPDS Card No. 37–1492. The diffraction peaks observed for the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5GQD-30 MoS2/BMO photocatalysts correspond to both MoS2 and Bi2MoO6. Additionally, the peak positions of both MoS2 and Bi2MoO6 were unchanged after uniform mixing of MoS2 with the Bi2MoO6 flowers, indicating no change in the crystal structure of the formed MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite. Notably, the diffraction peaks arising from the GQDs in the XRD pattern of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO were not identifiable owing to the low GQD content in the composite system. No other impurities were observed in the composite materials, suggesting that the final 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO photocatalyst composite was highly pure.

The Raman spectra obtained for pristine Bi2MoO6, MoS2, 30 MoS2/BMO, and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composites are presented in Fig. 2 B and clearly reveal the structures of the materials. The Raman peaks observed for pristine Bi2MoO6 appear at 329, 351, 403, 720, 798, and 853 cm−1. The bands at 720 (Eu mode), 798 (A1g mode), and 853 (A2u mode) cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the MoO6 octahedra (A1g + A2u + Eu) in Bi2MoO6, whereas the Raman bands observed at 329, 351, and 403 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the Bi–O bonds (Eu symmetric bending) [42–46]. In the spectrum of pure MoS2, multiple Raman bands were observed in the range of 200–900 cm−1. The typical Raman bands of MoS2 appear at 382 and 405 cm−1, corresponding to the in-plane E2g 1 and out-of-plane A1g vibrations, respectively. The Raman-shift difference between the in-plane and out-of-plane vibrations approximates monolayer- (20 cm−1) and multilayer-type (25 cm−1) MoS2. This difference was ≈ 23 cm−1, suggesting that the MoS2 petals were multilayered [47–49]. The spectrum of the 30 MoS2/BMO composite contained the characteristic peaks of Bi2MoO6, and only two Raman peaks (marked with *) corresponding to MoS2 were observed owing to the overlapping peaks from MoS2 and Bi2MoO6. For 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, sharp peaks at 1362 and 1601 cm−1 were observed for the GQDs corresponding to the D- (disordered sp2 carbon) and G-band of ordered graphite. The peak intensity ratio of the D to G bands can serve as a measure of the structural disorder and was calculated as 0.85, originating from the defects associated with the system [50,51]. In addition, the Raman spectra of 3-, 5-, and 10-GQD-30MoS2/BMO were also compared (see Figure S1 ); it can be seen that the positions of the D- and G-band remain the same. However, the Raman bands were shallow in the 3 GQD-30MoS2/BMO spectrum compared to those of the 5- and 10-GQD-30MoS2/BMO catalyst systems. The Raman results also confirm the presence of GQDs in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO photocatalyst.

Fig. 3
A shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms obtained for pure Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite. The measured specific surface areas were 21.25, 35.63, and 45.12 m2/g for Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite, respectively. The arrangement of the MoS2 flowers on Bi2MoO6 and the GQDs results in an increased surface area compared to both pristine flower forms. An evident increase in the surface area was observed in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite, indicating the high availability of the surface for the photocatalytic reaction.

The surface chemical compositions of the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composites were analyzed using XPS, and the survey spectra along with the spectra of the individual elements are presented in Fig. 3 B–F. The survey spectra of the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 3 B. The narrow-scanned C1s spectrum of 30 MoS2/BMO exhibits only two peaks located at binding energies of 284.8 and 286.1 eV, corresponding to the C–C and C–O bond originating from adventitious carbon in the composite. However, 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO exhibits three peaks at binding energies of 282.3, 285.8, and 288.3 eV, which were attributed to the C–C, C–OH, and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in C = O, respectively [52]. The C1s spectrum also confirms the presence of GQDs in the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst (Fig. 3 C). The O1s spectrum of 30 MoS2/BMO could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.4, 529.9, and 531.0 eV, which were attributed to the lattice oxygen atoms in Bi2MoO6, adsorbed oxygen molecules, and hydroxyl groups, respectively (Fig. 3 D). Conversely, peaks at similar binding energies of 529.5 and 530.1 eV were also observed in the spectrum of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, with one additional peak at 532.1 eV originating from the O = C–OH/C–OH groups. The peaks located at binding energies of 228.9 and 232.1 eV in 30 MoS2/BMO and 228.8 and 232.3 eV in 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO correspond to Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2, respectively, from the Mo4+ oxidation state of MoS2. In the spectra of both catalysts, a small S2s peak is apparent at 226.2 eV (Fig. 3 E). The deconvolution of the Mo3d spectra also revealed peaks located at binding energies of 231.1 and 235.3 eV, corresponding to the + 6 oxidation state of Mo in Bi2MoO6 in 30 MoS2/BMO and 231.5 and 235.4 eV in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite [53,54].

The peaks observed at 158.8 and 164.2 eV in the 30 MoS2/BMO spectrum corresponded to the Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2 species (Fig. 3 F), respectively. Conversely, the peaks at 159.1 and 164.4 eV were attributed to Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2, respectively [42,53]. The peaks observed for Bi 4f in the 30 MoS2/BMO spectrum confirmed the trivalent electronic state of Bi. The atomic percentage ratio of Bi:Mo:O elements was approximately 1.96:0.98:6.29, corresponding to a Bi2MoO6 elemental proportion in the compounds. A slight shift of the characteristic peaks of Bi4f in spectra of the 30 MoS2/BMO and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composites was observed, which was possibly due to the electronic transition between the GQDs, MoS2, and Bi2MoO6. Spectral overlap between S2p and Bi4f was observed, as for the Mo3d and S2s spectra. The S2p spectra were deconvoluted to spin–orbit doublets at binding energies of 161.2 and 162.3 eV in 30 MoS2/BMO and 161.4 and 162.7 eV in 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively. The peaks confirmed the divalent sulfide oxidation state that arises from S2– in MoS2 [55].

The optical absorption of Bi2MoO6, MoS2, 30 MoS2/BMO, and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO was determined using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4
A. For pristine Bi2MoO6, the onset absorption was observed at ≈ 470 nm, which was attributed to the transition of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) upon light excitation. In contrast, the absorption was in the range of 450–700 nm for MoS2, with detectable peaks at 674 and 621 nm [56]. For the 30 MoS2/BMO composite, the absorption peak of Bi2MoO6 in the visible-light region was wider and more intense than that of pristine Bi2MoO6. Conversely, 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO exhibited increased absorption in the visible-light region (500–800 nm) compared to the 30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. The broad absorbance peaks below 300 nm correspond to the π–π* transition in aromatic sp2 domains of GQDs, while the n–π*transition occurs at 340 nm [57]. Thus, as observed from the figure, absorption occurs over the whole range with a slight red shift in wavelength owing to MoS2 and GQD conjugation. Moreover, the band gap energies (Eg) of MoS2 and Bi2MoO6 were calculated from the Tauc plot of (αhν)2 versus hν, in which the curve was extrapolated to the X-axis to yield the band gap of the material. The values obtained for MoS2 and the Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst were 1.72 and 2.8 eV, respectively, which were in good agreement with those reported in the literature [58,59]. Before proposing the charge-transfer mechanism, Mott–Schottky (M−S) plots were constructed to determine the band-edge potentials and band structure in order to understand the type of charge transfer occurring between Bi2MoO6 and MoS2. A detailed calculation of the band positions and a description of the analysis are provided in the Supporting Information ( Figure S2 ).

According to the photoresponse measurements (Fig. 4 C), the photocurrent density observed for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst was much higher than that of pristine Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the 30 MoS2/BMO composite, indicating rapid charge transfer at the interface and enhanced separation efficiency after decorating the 30 MoS2/BMO composite surface with GQDs. The response current of the 30 MoS2/BMO composite was higher with respect to individual oxide systems, signifying the electron–hole separation efficiency between MoS2 and Bi2MoO6 and affirming the strong interfacial contact in this composite. The electrochemical impedance spectra of the photocatalysts also supported these results. In general, the semicircular arcs in the Nyquist plots with diameters along Z’ in the X-axis relate to the charge-transfer resistance in the catalyst system (Fig. 4 D). In other words, the generated electron–hole pair separation is influenced by the lower interfacial resistance of the catalyst, which is reflected by the semicircular arcs; a smaller arc radius indicates a faster charge-transfer process and better material performance [60]. The photoelectrode constructed from the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst exhibited a smaller arc radius compared to the other catalysts, which was attributed to the lower recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers, indicating a higher rate of charge transfer in the system. The trend observed for charge transfer in the fabricated photoelectrodes was Bi2MoO6 < MoS2 < 30MoS2/BMO < 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO. The results also revealed an obvious decrease in the resistance after incorporating GQDs, which was supported by the high photocurrent density. This resulted in good transfer efficiency with minimal resistance and prolonged the lifetime of the electron–hole pairs, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic process. Additionally, the photoluminescence spectral data supported the enhanced charge separation in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst, as shown in Figure S3 .

The photocatalytic activities of the binary- and ternary-composite photocatalysts toward the degradation of ofloxacin, a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, were studied. Initially, the optimum amount of MoS2 in the MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst was evaluated. The degradation of ofloxacin under light irradiation was carried out without any catalyst to understand the effect of light on the photocatalytic activity. Even after light irradiation for 100 min, there was a negligible effect on the antibiotic, demonstrating the high stability of ofloxacin. In addition, pristine Bi2MoO6 and the MoS2 photocatalyst were tested in the catalytic degradation of ofloxacin; the extent of degradation was approximately 40 and 32%, respectively. Prior to the degradation process, an adsorption–desorption equilibrium experiment was also carried out in the absence of light, yielding approximately 12 and 5% degradation in the presence of Bi2MoO6 and MoS2, respectively. Since ofloxacin is a highly resistant organic pollutant, <50% degradation was observed after 100 min when the single semiconducting materials were used (Fig. 5
A). The amount of MoS2 added to Bi2MoO6 had a significant impact on both the adsorption and photocatalytic process. This could be attributed to effective charge-carrier separation owing to the heterojunction formed between the semiconducting materials as a result of favoring band positions influencing charge separation, as observed in the band structure plotted from the Tauc plot and Mott–Schottky data ( Figure S2 ). Notably, increasing the amount of MoS2 from 10 to 30 wt% increased both the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance. The highest ofloxacin degradation of 98.4% was achieved using 30 wt% MoS2 in the binary MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst owing to the increased light absorption by MoS2, as evident in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 A). However, increasing the amount of MoS2 above 30% decreased the reactivity because excessive MoS2 contributed to charge-carrier recombination on the surface of the Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, thereby inhibiting the photochemical reaction.

Since the optimized 30 MoS2/BMO binary-composite photocatalyst demonstrated the highest reactivity, a ternary-composite photocatalyst was prepared by adding various concentrations of GQDs to this catalyst. Fig. 5 C shows the results of the photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin in the presence of 3 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, and 10 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO for comparison with the 30 MoS2/BMO binary-composite photocatalyst. The addition of different concentrations of GQDs to 30 MoS2/BMO did not significantly affect the adsorption process; however, its photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of ofloxacin was significantly enhanced, reducing the time required for degradation. The 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation of 99.5% over 100 min, where > 95% of the degradation occurred in the first 60 min. However, increasing the concentration of GQDs was detrimental to the degradation process, which could be attributed to the excess surface coverage by the GQDs and local agglomeration of MoS2 and Bi2MoO6, hindering the charge-transfer process during the photocatalytic reaction. This led to blockage of active sites in the catalyst system, thereby reducing the overall catalytic activity to below that of 30 MoS2/BMO. Compared with the binary MoS2/BMO composite photocatalysts (50–100%), the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of the ternary GQD-MoS2/BMO photocatalysts were in the range of 85–100% over the same reaction period, indicating their superior efficiency. Furthermore, to ensure complete degradation of ofloxacin—even on the photocatalyst surface—the catalyst was characterized using FTIR spectroscopy after adsorption and again after degradation, and the spectra were compared with those of the pristine catalyst and ofloxacin. A detailed explanation is presented in the Supporting Information ( Figure S4 ).

The reaction rates observed using the binary and ternary photocatalysts were obtained by fitting the experimental points to a pseudo-first-order kinetic model using the following expression: ln C0/C = kt, where k is the pseudo-first-order kinetic rate constant (min−1), and C and Co are the initial concentrations of ofloxacin and ofloxacin at a time (t), respectively. The fitted kinetic models for the binary- and ternary-composite photocatalysts are illustrated in Fig. 5 B and 5D, respectively. For all catalysts, the rate constants were obtained from plots of ln (C/Co) vs. t owing to the reasonable correlation coefficients (R2) of > 90% for data points up to the 60 min mark (with the exception of 30 MoS2/BMO, for which R2 > 80%). The photocatalytic degradation efficiency and kinetic rate constants along with their corresponding correlation coefficients are presented in Table S1. The kinetic rate constant was the highest (3.3 × 10–2 min−1) for the ternary 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite catalyst, which was 14.3- and 1.8-fold higher than those of pristine Bi2MoO6 (0.23 × 10–2 min−1) and 30 MoS2/BMO (1.8 × 10–2 min−1). Further experiments were conducted to analyze the catalyst content and effect of ofloxacin concentration on photocatalytic degradation efficiency. These studies reveal that ≈ 0.66 g/L of the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO catalyst can effectively degrade a 10 mg L–1 solution of ofloxacin with a photodegradation efficiency of > 95%. The highest concentration of ofloxacin degraded using 1 g/L catalyst was 20 mg/L, which had an efficiency similar to that observed for 10 mg/L ofloxacin over the same reaction time ( Figure S5 ).

Remarkably, 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO exhibited the best degradation efficiency toward ofloxacin. Adding persulfate to the reaction system can boost the reaction kinetics through the generation of additional reactive species, influencing the overall photochemical reaction [61]. Therefore, the use of persulfate with the optimized composite photocatalyst was also evaluated to investigate the activation of persulfate and its influence on photodegradation efficiency. Fig. 5 E shows the concentration of persulfate, which ranged from 0.25 to 5 mM in the photocatalytic reaction system employing the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. Persulfate was activated in the presence of the composite photocatalyst, and the photodegradation of ofloxacin was > 80% in the first 20 min of the photochemical reaction, where 3 mM of persulfate increased the reaction rate. The persulfate concentration plays a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation process involving the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. The degradation efficiency was highest in the presence of 3 mM of persulfate, and the reaction rate increased; the kinetic rate constant was 10.7 × 10–2 min−1, which was ≈ 3.24-fold higher than that using only the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. Notably, adding only 3 mM of persulfate without light irradiation enhanced the catalytic activity of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO toward persulfate. However, the degradation of ofloxacin is slower compared to the combined photocatalytic and persulfate system. The results also suggest that adding > 3 mM of persulfate has a detrimental effect on the photodegradation efficiency; adding 5 mM of persulfate to the reaction mixture led to a 2.27-fold decrease in the reaction rate compared with adding 3 mM. A higher persulfate concentration requires a higher number of electrons for activation. The lack of electron photo-production can also affect the activation process of persulfate ions at higher concentrations because they may undergo further chemical reactions (as shown in Eq. (1) and (2)), reducing the active-species viability. Another possibility is the clogging of the surface of the catalyst with persulfate, which can prevent light from reaching the catalyst, thereby reducing the photodegradation efficiency.
(1) S2O8 2– + SO4·– → SO4 2– + S2O8·–
(2) S2O8 2– + ·OH → OH– + S2O8·–

The excellent performance of the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO photocatalyst can be attributed to the improved light absorption, surface adsorption, and photochemical reaction, where enhanced charge separation is predicted to result from the higher photocurrent density compared with that of the binary composite and individual material systems as well the low charge-transfer resistance, as evident from the impedance measurements. The stability of the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite catalyst was also investigated by conducting consecutive catalytic runs in the presence of 3 mM of persulfate under visible-light irradiation; the experimental results are shown in Fig. 5 F. The photocatalytic performance was not affected over five consecutive catalytic cycles, indicating the high stability of the as-prepared photocatalyst. The X-ray diffraction pattern and FESEM image of the spent catalyst are presented in the Supplementary Information ( Figure S6 ).

The time profiles for the reduction of hexavalent chromium using the different catalysts (Bi2MoO6, MoS2, 30 MoS2/BMO, and 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO) were obtained and analyzed in terms of the photocatalytic reduction efficiency under visible-light irradiation. The profiles were plotted using the UV–visible spectra of photochemically reacted Cr(VI) solutions obtained at different time intervals at a wavelength of 540 nm. Fig. 6
A shows significant adsorption (40%) using the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst, where the lowest amount of adsorption occurs on the MoS2 surface (9%). Notably, the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) over the binary 30 MoS2/BMO composite catalyst proceeded with a removal efficiency of ≈ 92% over 50 min, with 19% adsorption on the surface of the catalyst. Compared to the single-component systems of Bi2MoO6 (79%) and MoS2 (72%), the degradation efficiency of the binary-composite photocatalyst was significantly higher, suggesting effective separation of the charge carriers and an increased photogenerated electron availability for the reaction. 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO exhibited the highest photodegradation efficiency, with ≈ 100% removal efficiency of Cr(VI) and > 95% degradation in the first 30 min of the photochemical reaction. Thus, the presence of GQDs enhances the reduction reactivity, which is reflected in the shorter time required to remove Cr(VI) completely from the aqueous solution. Notably, the Cr(VI) reduction using Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 was 0.79- and 0.72-fold higher, respectively, than that of the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst over 60 min. Since the photochemical reduction of Cr(VI) mainly occurs on the surface of the catalyst, the adsorption of Cr(VI) on the catalyst surface also has a major contribution to the excellent reduction ability of the optimized catalyst.

The kinetic profile and corresponding plots of ln(C/Co) vs. time are presented in Fig. 6 B. Good linearity was observed for the pseudo-first-order fitting of the Cr(VI) reduction process using the different catalysts. The correlation coefficients (R2) of the fitted data were > 95 for all the photocatalysts. The kinetic rate constants were obtained from the slope of the ln(C/Co) vs. time plot by linear fitting of the experimental data; the values corresponding to the different catalysts are plotted in Fig. 6 C and tabulated in Table S2. The kinetic rate constants observed for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst was 15.25 × 10–2 min−1, which was approximately 5.95- and 6.96-fold that of the pristine Bi2MoO6 (2.56 × 10–2 min−1) and MoS2 (2.19 × 10–2 min−1) catalysts, respectively. The reaction rate using 30 MoS2/BMO (3.75 × 10–2 min−1) was 4.06-fold lower than that of the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO photocatalyst. The photocatalytic reduction process using the optimized GQD-decorated 30 MoS2/BMO system was faster than the photochemical reaction, and less energy was consumed in the reduction process. A comparative table presenting the results of the reduction of Cr(VI) in aqueous solution using different catalysts, including removal efficiencies and rate constants, are listed in Table S3 to highlight the advantages of the developed catalytic system. Based on the results of this study, the developed systems are either better than or equally efficient as several established catalytic systems used to reduce Cr(VI). Multiple pollutants coexist in real wastewater samples, which affect the photo-oxidation/reduction ability of the catalyst. Therefore, experiments were conducted using 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO in the presence of both the micropollutants in the photochemical system ( Figure S7 ). The degradation of ofloxacin is restricted in the presence of Cr(VI) owing to the blocking of the active sites by the adsorbed Cr(VI) on the photocatalyst surface, revealing its affinity for Cr(VI). A detailed analysis is presented in the Supporting Information.

To compare and analyze the performance of the prepared photocatalysts with other catalysts reported in the literature, a quantitative measurement factor termed the photonic removal frequency (PRF) was calculated as follows [62]:

The PRF values were calculated for several Bi2MoO6-based heterojunctions and Z-scheme photocatalysts for the degradation of ofloxacin and Cr(VI) and are presented in Table S3. In the present case, 99.5% of a 10 mg/L solution of ofloxacin was degraded within ≈ 100 min by using the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. Therefore, the PRF calculated from the data was 10 × 0.995/150 W/100 min = 0.00066 mg/W/min/L. For the reduction of Cr(VI), the PRF calculated from the data was 10 × 0.999/150 W/50 min = 0.0013 mg/W/min/L. The obtained PRF values were superior to those of several previously reported catalysts used to degrade ofloxacin and reduce Cr(VI).

Recycling experiments using the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst were carried out to evaluate the stability of the material during reduction under visible-light irradiation. The catalyst retains > 95% of its initial activity even after five consecutive catalytic cycles. Approximately 2.3% of the reduction efficiency was lost, which may be due to the incomplete desorption of the converted Cr(III) from the catalyst surface and catalyst loss during the separation and post-treatment process [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge-transfer mechanism in the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO ternary-composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
An investigation of the active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin was carried out using various scavenging reagents (Fig. 7
A). 1 mM of tert-butyl alcohol (t-BA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and 1,4-benzoquinone (1,4-BQ) was used to capture the hydroxyl radicals (OH·), photogenerated holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (O2·–), respectively, formed during the degradation of aqueous ofloxacin solution using the ternary-composite photocatalyst. The experimental results revealed that the degradation of ofloxacin was significantly hindered in the presence of 1,4-BQ, a superoxide radical scavenger, suggesting that the reactivity is mainly derived from these reactive species. Photogenerated holes also assisted in the process owing to the VB of Bi2MoO6, as most of the photogenerated electrons may be recombined at the interface of Bi2MoO6 and MoS2. In addition, the production of superoxide radicals on the conduction band of Bi2MoO6 was not possible owing to the unfavorable CB position for converting O2 to O2 ·– (–0.33 eV). Although the hydroxyl radicals also contributed, their role was less prominent than that of the superoxide radicals. Hydroxyl radical production is only possible at the VB of Bi2MoO6 via the photogenerated holes because the MoS2 VB is much more negative than the potential required to convert a hydroxyl ion into a radical (OH–/OH·, 2.38 eV). In fact, the charge transfer between the CB of MoS2 to Bi2MoO6 may not even occur because the CB of Bi2MoO6 may not transform O2 to O2 ·– owing to the more positive CB potential of Bi2MoO6 than the required O2/O2 ·– potential, eliminating the possibility of type-II heterojunction charge transfer among the catalysts. Therefore, it may be proposed that superoxide radicals, which are major reactive species in the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO catalyst, are generated from the CB of MoS2 via a Z-scheme charge-transfer mechanism between Bi2MoO6 and MoS2. In addition, the transfer of photogenerated electrons is improved in the presence of GQDs in the composite system, which maintain the reaction chain for the enhanced photocatalytic reaction, as observed from the significant decrease in the reaction rate in the presence of 1,4-BQ (0.39 × 10–2 min−1) compared with AO (0.80 × 10–2 min−1) and t-BA (1.17 × 10−2 min−1), as shown in the inset of Fig. 7 A. Although the scavenger studies revealed a major contribution from both superoxide and hydroxyl radicals during the degradation of ofloxacin, the in-situ formation of both species over the different as-prepared catalysts could provide insights into the types of radicals formed over the catalysts. The in-situ formation of these radicals was confirmed using a probing technique involving terephthalic acid [64] and a nitroblue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) test [65], as presented in Figure S8 .

Another technique that can help elucidate the efficiency of charge separation and transfer is solid-state EPR spectroscopy. Unpaired free electrons can be detected using EPR spectroscopy. In addition, an increase in the surface oxygen vacancies is represented by a high-intensity signal in EPR spectra, which increases the carrier density and facilitates the transport of charge carriers in the system. Fig. 7 B shows the EPR spectra obtained for the optimized 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO composite photocatalyst. Note that no signal was obtained under dark conditions. The g-value at 2.0068 corresponds to the superoxide radical formed at the vacant site of surface oxygen [66]. Upon increasing the irradiation time, the EPR signal intensity increased, suggesting the trapping of photogenerated electrons on the surface of the catalyst. It should be noted that the displacement of oxygen atoms led vacant oxygen sites on the surface of the catalyst owing to strong hydrogen bonding with the GQDs [67]. The results indicate structural defects owing to interactions between Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and the GQDs, which promote the generation of photoelectrons in the composite catalyst system.

In accordance with the band-energy diagram obtained from the combined data of the Mott–Schottky/Tauc plots ( Figure S2
/ Fig. 4B) along with the scavenger experiments and in-situ probing results, a plausible photocatalytic mechanism accounting for the enhanced performance was deduced for the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO catalytic system, as presented in Fig. 7 C. Upon illumination with visible light, pristine Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 in 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO were excited to the photogenerated holes in the VB and electrons (e–) in the CB. The photogenerated holes in the VB of Bi2MoO6 undergo a reaction with the hydroxyl ions and photogenerated reactive hydroxyl radical species owing to the more positive VB edge (2.58 eV) of Bi2MoO6 compared with the OH–/OH· oxidation potential (2.38 eV). As confirmed by the FESEM and TEM images, the close interfacial contact between Bi2MoO6 and MoS2 allows the photogenerated electrons in the CB of Bi2MoO6 to transfer to the VB of MoS2 for recombination with the photogenerated holes in the MoS2 VB owing to the potential difference. The electrons in the CB of MoS2 can transfer to the GQDs on its surface owing to their high affinity for electrons [68]. Additionally, the optimized 5 GQD-30MoS2/BMO composite has a large surface area, which facilitates the generation of active catalytic sites for the adsorption of micropollutants and heavy metals, furthering boosting the catalytic process through promoting faster surface reactions than those occurring in the individual material systems. The transferred electron then reacts with the adsorbed oxygen molecules to produce superoxide radicals on the surface. In addition, GQDs on the binary catalyst surface perform multiple roles; one of their main roles is the extension of the visible-light absorptivity of 5 GQD-30 MoS2/BMO, and another is the acceleration of electron transfer in the 30 MoS2/BMO catalytic system. The latter suggests that electron transfer can occur through the GQDs located at the interface of the catalyst, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. A detailed reaction mechanism for ofloxacin photo-oxidation and Cr(VI) photoreduction is provided in section 2 of the Supporting Information.

The decoration of GQDs over a chemically mixed MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite catalyst was successfully carried out. The resulting ternary GQD-MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite exhibited excellent photocatalytic properties owing to faster electron transport and the synergistic effect between the GQDs and MoS2/Bi2MoO6, extending the visible-light absorption to cover a greater range than that of pristine Bi2MoO6, MoS2, and binary MoS2/Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalysts. A direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism was observed in 5 GQD-30 MoS2/Bi2MoO6, facilitating the electron–hole pair separation and transfer to photoproduce reactive species. Approximately > 95% ofloxacin degradation and Cr(VI) reduction occurred using the ternary photocatalyst in 100 and 30 min, respectively. The degradation of ofloxacin was improved in the presence of persulfate, achieving > 80% degradation in the first 30 min of the photochemical reaction. Owing to the scavenging experiments and probing techniques (terephthalic acid and nitroblue tetrazolium) conducted in this study, both the superoxide radicals and photogenerated holes were responsible for the high oxidation and reduction efficiencies of ofloxacin and hexavalent chromium, suggesting that the inhibition of charge-carrier pair recombination occurred during the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the 5 GQD-30 MoS2/Bi2MoO6 catalyst exhibited superior performance in the cycling tests, which is necessary for its practical application. GQDs act as an excellent supporting catalyst in the binary-composite catalyst system and significantly improve the photocatalytic degradation efficiency toward hazardous aqueous contaminants.
